title: Candy Making
content:
CLASSIFICATION

Crystalline: Fondants, fudges—smooth, creamy, fine crystals
Non-Crystalline: Butterscotch, caramels—no crystals formed

TEMPERATURE STAGES

See Temperature & Timing Guide section for complete candy temperature chart.

GENERAL PROCEDURES

Equipment: Smooth inner surface saucepan with straight sides and flat bottom (heavy to minimize scorching). Heavy spoon with wooden handle. Shallow pan for molding: fondants—rinsed in cold water; marshmallows—dusted with powdered sugar; other candies—well-buttered.

Heat Regulation: Low enough flame to prevent creeping up sides and burning sugar. Cover entire bottom for even heating.

Preventing Crystals: Wipe sides of saucepan with damp cloth during cooking to remove sugar grains. Do not scrape saucepan as candy is poured out.

Beating: For fluffy candies (divinity, sea foam): beat stiff egg whites; pour hot syrup in slowly while beating constantly (cooks the white). For creamy candies: cool to room temperature; beat until stiff enough to hold shape. Signs of correct stage: loses gloss, softens, becomes warmer to hand, lighter in color, suddenly stiffens. Insufficient beating = large crystals; not cooled before beating = coarse texture. If large crystals form: add water and recook.

Cold Water Tests: Use fresh cold water for each test. Remove pan from heat to prevent overcooking. Pour ½ teaspoon candy into ½ cup cold water; pick up and note consistency. See Temperature Guide for test descriptions.
